title: Empirical and Molecular Formulas
subtitle: From mass percentages to the actual molecule
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: explore the concept of the mole and relate this to Avogadro's constant to describe, calculate and manipulate numbers of particles, chemical amounts and masses
calculate percentage composition by mass of multi-element compounds
inquiryQuestion: How are measurements made in chemistry?
lessonIntent: Students can derive an empirical formula from mass-percentage data and convert it to a molecular formula using the compound's molar mass.
examSkill: Apply the four-step procedure to convert percentage-composition data into an empirical formula, then scale to a molecular formula using M(molecular) ÷ M(empirical).

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Chemistry. Today, empirical and molecular formulas. Let's get into it.

scenes.caption: Empirical and Molecular Formulas (Year 11 Chemistry, Module 2, Lesson 3)

scenes.heading: What molecule is this?
scenes.body: 40% C, 6.7% H, 53.3% O. Glucose? Formaldehyde? Acetic acid? All match.
scenes.callout: Mass data alone, can tell you the molecule.
scenes.caption: Mass-percentage data is the fingerprint of a compound.

voiceover.text: OK, here's the problem. A chemist examines a mystery white powder. The mass spectrometer says forty percent carbon, six-point-seven percent hydrogen, fifty-three-point-three percent oxygen. That's the percentage composition. Just the masses. So what is the molecule? Is it glucose? Formaldehyde? Sugar? Acetic acid? It could be any of them. They all share the same ratio of atoms. By the end of this video, you'll know exactly how to crack this open. Let's go.

scenes.heading: Empirical vs molecular
scenes.body: Empirical formula gives you the simplest whole-number ratio. Molecular formula gives you the actual count.

bullets.text: Glucose: C₆H₁₂O₆. Six carbons, twelve hydrogens, six oxygens.
Formaldehyde: CH₂O. One carbon, two hydrogens, one oxygen.
Same empirical formula. Different molecules entirely.

scenes.secondary: Molecular formula = empirical formula × n, where n is a whole number.
scenes.caption: Empirical formula is the simplest ratio. Molecular formula is the actual count.

voiceover.text: OK, so what's the difference between empirical and molecular? The empirical formula is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms. Molecular formula is the actual count per molecule. Let me show you why this matters. Take glucose. Molecular formula. C six, H twelve, O six. Its empirical formula. CH two O. Now look at formaldehyde. Its molecular formula. Also CH two O. Same ratio, different molecules. The molecular formula always equals the empirical formula times some whole number n. For glucose, n is six. For formaldehyde, n is one. Empirical tells you the ratio. Molecular tells you the actual count.

scenes.heading: Three terms to lock in
scenes.callout: Percentages add to one hundred. Ratios reduce to whole numbers.
scenes.caption: Percentage composition is the input. Empirical and molecular formulas are the outputs.

voiceover.text: OK, let's lock in the vocabulary. First. Percentage composition. The mass of each element, written as a percentage of the total mass. So if a compound is forty percent carbon, that means in a hundred-gram sample, forty grams are carbon. Next. Empirical formula. The simplest whole-number ratio of atoms. The reduced form. And finally. Molecular formula. The actual count of each atom in one molecule. These three are connected. Percentage composition is what you measure. Empirical formula is the first step. Molecular formula is where we want to end up.

bullets.text: Percentage composition: the mass of each element as a percentage of the total mass.
Empirical formula: the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms.
Molecular formula: the actual count of each atom per molecule.

scenes.heading: The four-step procedure
scenes.body: From percentages to empirical formula, in four moves.

bullets.text: 1. Assume you have 100 g of the compound.
2. Convert each mass to moles using molar mass.
3. Divide every mole value by the smallest.
4. Clear any decimals by multiplying through.

scenes.secondary: Then n = M(molecular) ÷ M(empirical) scales the empirical formula to the molecular one.
scenes.callout: 100 g turns every percentage straight into grams.
scenes.caption: Four-step procedure: assume 100 g → moles → divide by smallest → whole numbers.

voiceover.text: OK, here's the procedure that always works. Four steps. Step one. Assume you have exactly one hundred grams of the compound. That turns every percentage straight into grams. Forty percent carbon becomes forty grams of carbon. Easy. Step two. Convert each mass to moles using the molar mass from the periodic table. Step three. Divide every mole value by the smallest one. That gives you the ratio. Step four. If you get clean decimals, like one-point-five or one-point-three-three, multiply everything through by a small integer to clear them. One-point-five becomes three when you times by two. One-point-three-three becomes four when you times by three. That gives you the empirical formula. Then to find the molecular formula, take n equals the molecular molar mass divided by the empirical molar mass. Multiply through. Done.

scenes.heading: Worked example
scenes.question: A compound is 40.0% C, 6.7% H, 53.3% O and has a molar mass of 180 g mol⁻¹. Find its empirical and molecular formulas.
scenes.coachNote: Assume 100 g. Mass percentages become grams.
steps: Assume 100 g: 40.0 g C, 6.7 g H, 53.3 g O
Divide by smallest (3.33): C = 1, H = 2, O = 1
Empirical formula: CH₂O. M(empirical) = 12.01 + 2(1.008) + 16.00 = 30.03 g mol⁻¹
scenes.caption: From % data to glucose: CH₂O empirical, C₆H₁₂O₆ molecular.

voiceover.text: Let's work through it. The question reads: a compound is forty percent carbon, six-point-seven percent hydrogen, fifty-three-point-three percent oxygen. Molar mass one hundred and eighty grams per mole. Find the empirical and molecular formulas. OK, first move. Assume a hundred-gram sample. So that's forty grams of carbon, six-point-seven grams of hydrogen, fifty-three-point-three grams of oxygen. Step two, convert each to moles. Forty divided by twelve-point-zero-one is three-point-three-three moles of carbon. Six-point-seven divided by one-point-zero-zero-eight is six-point-six-five moles of hydrogen. Fifty-three-point-three divided by sixteen is three-point-three-three moles of oxygen. Step three, divide everything by the smallest, which is three-point-three-three. Carbon, one. Hydrogen, two. Oxygen, one. No decimals to clean up. The empirical formula is CH two O. Now the molecular formula. The empirical molar mass is twelve plus two times one-point-zero-zero-eight plus sixteen. That's thirty-point-zero-three. The actual molar mass is one hundred and eighty. So n equals one hundred and eighty divided by thirty-point-zero-three, which is six. Multiply every subscript by six. The molecular formula is C six H twelve O six. That's glucose.

scenes.heading: Two costly mistakes
scenes.body: Mistake 1: rounding decimals before checking what they round to. A ratio of 1 to 1.5 is NOT 1 to 1. Multiply by 2 to get 2 to 3.
scenes.secondary: Mistake 2: confusing empirical with molecular. The empirical formula is the simplest ratio. The molecular formula is the actual one. Always check using the molar mass.
scenes.callout: Decimals only round when they're close to whole numbers.
scenes.caption: Don't round decimals like 1.5 or 1.33 to whole numbers. Multiply through instead.

voiceover.text: Right, two mistakes that cost real marks. Mistake one. Rounding too early. If your ratio comes out one to one-point-five, do not round that down to one to one. That's a different compound entirely. Multiply both by two to clear it. So one to one-point-five becomes two to three. One to one-point-three-three becomes three to four. One to one-point-two-five becomes four to five. The pattern. The decimal tells you what to multiply by. Mistake two. Confusing empirical with molecular. The empirical formula is the simplest ratio. The molecular formula is the actual one. They are often different. Always finish the job. Use the molar mass to find n. Then multiply through. Don't stop at the empirical formula unless that's all the question asks for.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: A compound is 80.0% carbon and 20.0% hydrogen. Its molar mass is 30 g mol⁻¹. Find the molecular formula.
answerSteps: Assume 100 g: 80 g C, 20 g H
scenes.caption: Find C₂H₆ from 80% C and 20% H with molar mass 30 g mol⁻¹.

voiceover.text: Quick check. A compound is eighty percent carbon and twenty percent hydrogen. Molar mass thirty grams per mole. Find the molecular formula. Pause and work it through. Right, let's check it. Assume one hundred grams. So eighty grams of carbon, twenty grams of hydrogen. Moles. Eighty divided by twelve is six-point-six-seven. Twenty divided by one-point-zero-zero-eight is nineteen-point-eight-four. Divide by the smallest. Carbon, one. Hydrogen, about three. Empirical formula is CH three. Molar mass of CH three is twelve plus three times one-point-zero-zero-eight, which is fifteen. n equals thirty divided by fifteen, which is two. Multiply through. The molecular formula is C two H six. That's ethane.

scenes.heading: Five things to lock in
points: Empirical formula = simplest whole-number ratio of atoms.
Molecular formula = actual count per molecule.
Four-step procedure: 100 g → moles → divide by smallest → clear decimals.
n = M(molecular) ÷ M(empirical) scales empirical to molecular.
Never round decimals like 1.5 or 1.33 to whole numbers. Multiply through.
scenes.finalPrompt: Before every empirical-formula question: assume 100 g.
scenes.caption: Five takeaways: ratio vs actual, four-step procedure, molar-mass scaling.

voiceover.text: OK, let's lock in what we've covered. Five things to take away. First, the empirical formula is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms. Second, the molecular formula is the actual count of atoms in one molecule. Next, the four-step procedure. Assume one hundred grams. Convert to moles. Divide by the smallest. Then clear any decimals by multiplying through. To go from empirical to molecular, calculate n as the molecular molar mass divided by the empirical molar mass. And finally, never round decimals like one-point-five or one-point-three-three to whole numbers. Always multiply through. That's how you avoid losing marks. See you next lesson.
